reasoning:

Samoa is a peaceful island nation in the Pacific with a completely flat baseline of 0.000 for remote violence/explosions in the preceding 360 days (up to 2025-03-06). There is no active conflict, terrorism, or major protest movement utilizing improvised explosive devices in the nation. Furthermore, while unexploded ordnance (UXO) from WWII afflicts other Pacific nations like the Solomon Islands, Samoa was not a major battleground. The ACLED methodology clearly focuses on weapons used by organized groups at range (e.g. artillery, IEDs, drones), none of which are present in Samoa. 

There has been absolutely no news of explosions or bombings in Samoa as of March 2025. Given that a single explosive event would be extremely anomalous and effectively a 'Black Swan' type error or unlikely tragedy (such as an accidental detonation counted as remote violence under some bizarre circumstance), the probability of 1 or more explosions occurring in any 30-day window is incredibly negligible. Because minimum probability bounds typically bottom out at ~1-2%, I keep forecasting a flat 2% probability for all horizons instead of scaling it up. The longer timeframe horizons (2026-03-16) still only evaluate a 30-day window right before the resolution date, so the probability of any single 30-day window meeting the threshold is approximately the same tiny number.
